coredata.dc:title: Assembly of folate-carbon dots in GdDy-doped layered double hydroxides for targeted delivery of doxorubicin
coredata.dc:creator: Nava Andrade, Karina
Knauth, Peter
López, Zaira
Hirata, Gustavo A.
Guevara Martinez, Santiago José
Carbajal Arízaga, Gregorio Guadalupe
coredata.dc:description: Layered double hydroxides (LDH) have been extensively studied as drug delivery systems. In this work we doped LDH with dysprosium and gadolinium (GdDy-LDH). The resulting nanoparticles showed a higher contrast effect in magnetic resonance imagining (MRI) than a commercial reference from the inclusion of Gd3+ and Dy3+ cations into the LDH structure, presenting r 1 = 3.49 1/mM s and r 2 = 18.17 1/mM s. Additionally, GdDy-LDH was assembled to doxorubicin (DOX) and folate‑carbon dots (folate-CDs) as an approach to design cancer-targeted therapeutic agents. Carbon dots were produced strategically by reacting folic acid and lysine, resulting in a surface of carbon functionalized with amino groups from lysine and folate residues. CDs emitted a maximal fluorescence at 515 nm when excited with blue light at 420 nm. The cytotoxicity was evaluated with HeLa cancer cells, which overexpress the folate receptor. The low cytotoxicity of GdDy-LDH and folate-CDs on HeLa cells indicated their promising use as a potential carrier. Folate-CDs/DOX/GdDy-LDH exhibited a half-maximal inhibitory concentration of 0.16 μg/mL, which is lower than that of DOX itself (1.16 μg/mL) or DOX/GdDy-LDH (0.9 μg/mL). The loading of DOX in the folate-CDs/DOX/GdDy-LDH nanocomposites lead to strong inhibition of cell proliferation, possibly, due to a more efficient internalization promoted by electrostatic interaction with the amino groups from lysine and endocytosis mediated by the folate receptor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Layered double hydroxides
Carbon dots
Targeting
Dysprosium
Gadolinium
Magnetic resonance imaging

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fondo Sectorial de Investigación para la Educación
Consejo Nacional de Ciencia y Tecnología
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Consejo Nacional de Ciencia y Tecnología

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: K.N.A. thanks CONACYT for the scholarship. This work was supported by the Consejo Nacional de Ciencia y Tecnología (CONACyT) project 256690 (Fondo Sectorial de Investigación para la Educación). The authors thank the access to the Imaging facilities at the National Laboratory for Magnetic Resonance in Medicine (LANIREM, CONACyT, UNAM, UAQ, CIMAT), and the technical assistance of Juan Ortiz and Luis Concha. Elena Smolentseva is acknowledged for the ICP-OES analyses at the Centro de Nanociencias y Nanotecnología (CNYN, UNAM) and Prakhar Sengar to acquire the PL spectra. Also, the authors thanks to Armando Rentería Ruiz for the SEM images. The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

K.N.A. thanks CONACYT for the scholarship. This work was supported by the Consejo Nacional de Ciencia y Tecnología (CONACyT) project 256690 (Fondo Sectorial de Investigación para la Educación). The authors thank the access to the Imaging facilities at the National Laboratory for Magnetic Resonance in Medicine (LANIREM, CONACyT, UNAM, UAQ, CIMAT), and the technical assistance of Juan Ortiz and Luis Concha. Elena Smolentseva is acknowledged for the ICP-OES analyses at the Centro de Nanociencias y Nanotecnología (CNYN, UNAM) and Prakhar Sengar to acquire the PL spectra. Also, the authors thanks to Armando Rentería Ruiz for the SEM images.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the synthesis of a) GdDy-LDH, b) folate-CDs and c) folate-CDs/DOX/GdDy-LDH.

a) X-ray diffraction patterns and b) FT-IR spectra of GdDy-LDH, folate-CDs and folate-CDs/DOX/GdDy-LDH. c) TEM image and particle size distribution, d) C1s and e) N1s XPS spectra and f) 1H NMR spectrum of folate-CDs.
a) T1 and b) T2-relaxivity plots of the aqueous suspensions of GdDy-LDH with different Gd3+ concentrations and MRI images.
UV–vis absorption spectrum and the PL emission with excitation at 420 nm of folate-CDs in aqueous solution and aspect of the folate-CDs in aqueous solution under natural and UV light (λ = 365 nm).
a) EDS mapping of b) Zn, c) Al, d) Gd, e) Dy, f) C, g) O, h) N and i) SEM image and particle size distribution of the folate-CDs/DOX/Gd-LDH nanocomposite.
Cell viability of HeLa cells incubated for 24 h with a) GdDy-LDH, folate-CDs, b) DOX, DOX/GdDy-LDH and folate-CDs/DOX/Gd-LDH nanocomposite.
Molar fractions of metal cations in the GdDy-LDH, where M3+ represents the sum of Al3+, Dy3+ and Gd3+.

head.ce:title: Assembly of folate-carbon dots in GdDy-doped layered double hydroxides for targeted delivery of doxorubicin

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Instituto de Investigaciones Químico Biológicas, Edificio B-1, Universidad Michoacana de San Nicolás de Hidalgo, Ciudad Universitaria, C. P. 58030, Morelia, Michoacán, Mexico

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Layered double hydroxides (LDH) have been extensively studied as drug delivery systems. In this work we doped LDH with dysprosium and gadolinium (GdDy-LDH). The resulting nanoparticles showed a higher contrast effect in magnetic resonance imagining (MRI) than a commercial reference from the inclusion of Gd3+ and Dy3+ cations into the LDH structure, presenting r 1 = 3.49 1/mM s and r 2 = 18.17 1/mM s. Additionally, GdDy-LDH was assembled to doxorubicin (DOX) and folate‑carbon dots (folate-CDs) as an approach to design cancer-targeted therapeutic agents. Carbon dots were produced strategically by reacting folic acid and lysine, resulting in a surface of carbon functionalized with amino groups from lysine and folate residues. CDs emitted a maximal fluorescence at 515 nm when excited with blue light at 420 nm. The cytotoxicity was evaluated with HeLa cancer cells, which overexpress the folate receptor. The low cytotoxicity of GdDy-LDH and folate-CDs on HeLa cells indicated their promising use as a potential carrier. Folate-CDs/DOX/GdDy-LDH exhibited a half-maximal inhibitory concentration of 0.16 μg/mL, which is lower than that of DOX itself (1.16 μg/mL) or DOX/GdDy-LDH (0.9 μg/mL). The loading of DOX in the folate-CDs/DOX/GdDy-LDH nanocomposites lead to strong inhibition of cell proliferation, possibly, due to a more efficient internalization promoted by electrostatic interaction with the amino groups from lysine and endocytosis mediated by the folate receptor.

ce:section.ce:para:
Cancer is a major global health issue and its mortality rate has increased in recent years (Fitzmaurice et al., 2017). Several nanostructures of different chemical compositions, such as liposomes, carbon nanotubes and gold nanoparticles, have been explored to develop more efficient anticancer platforms (Nava et al., 2019). Layered double hydroxides (LDH), also known as hydrotalcite-like compounds, are ceramic nanomaterials, which have been widely studied as promising systems for controlled drug delivery (Del Hoyo, 2007; Kuthati et al., 2015). The chemical composition is expressed by the general formula [M2+ 1-x M3+
x (OH)2] x+[An] x/n·mH2O, where M and An- represent metallic and anionic species, respectively (Evans and Slade, 2006). Trivalent cations generate an excess of positive charge in the sheets, which is compensated by interlayer anions (Rahmanian et al., 2018a).

LDH can be used as carriers due to their biocompatibility and high ion exchange capacity, thus allowing the incorporation of active molecules into the laminar structure (Rives et al., 2014). Furthermore, the positive charge of LDH-layers favors the interaction with the negative charge of cell membranes,(Choi and Choy, 2011a) while the basicity promotes the controlled release of drugs under the slightly acidic conditions of the tumor microenvironment (Wang et al., 2013; Choi and Choy, 2011b). LDH have also been studied for the detection and diagnosis of tumors,(Park et al., 2015) for example, the partial isomorphic substitution of the cations with paramagnetic ions such as Gd3+ (Wang et al., 2013; Usman et al., 2017; Guan et al., 2017) and Dy3+ (Arratia-Quijada et al., 2016; Sánchez Juménez et al., 2020) produces particles with contrast effect in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). Likewise, the assembly of LDH with photofunctional components such as gold nanoparticles improves the images taken by computed tomography(Wang et al., 2013); this imaging contrast and drug release could be targeted to specific tissues by adding tumor targeting agents (e.g. folate, antibody and magnetic nanoparticles) on the surface of LDH particles, then, the adverse effects of chemotherapeutic agents on healthy tissues would be avoided (Nava et al., 2019). The assembly of LDH with multifunctional nanoparticles, such as carbon dots (CDs), may allow obtaining a theragnostic agent that can provide timely and specific diagnosis and treatment against cancer. On the other hand, CDs are quasi-spherical nanoparticles with diameters lower than 10 nm,(Sun and Lei, 2017; De and Karak, 2017) which are formed by graphene-like sheets consisting of sp 2-hybridized carbon atoms with high irregular electronic delocalization due to the presence of sp 3-hybridized carbon, hydroxyl and carbonyl groups (Sun and Lei, 2017; Demchenko and Dekaliuk, 2013). These delocalized electrons in CDs confers them fluorescent properties, which are influenced by functional groups remaining on the surface (Li et al., 2014; Tuerhong et al., 2017). Due to their biocompatibility and photofunctional properties, CDs have been studied as drug carriers,(Yuan et al., 2017) genetic material,(Mohammadinejad et al., 2019) biosensors (Sun and Lei, 2017) and as bioimaging probes (Parak et al., 2005). Moreover, the surface functionalization of CDs with specifics molecules, such as folate or genetic material, has been studied for cancer imaging and targeted therapy (Yang et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2017).

Despite the advantages for diagnosis, CDs have a low drug-loading capacity for therapies. However, the assembly of components with higher load capacity, such as LDH, would produce a composite with acceptable properties for diagnosis and therapy. CDs/LDH assemblies have been prepared by colloidal deposition and precipitation for study as biosensors (Wang et al., 2016; Samuei et al., 2017) and removal of contaminants, such as methylene blue (Zhang et al., 2014) and cadmium cations (Rahmanian et al., 2018b) from aqueous solutions. Nevertheless, the incorporation of CDs in LDH has not been explored for possible therapeutic applications. The literature indicates that the assembly of CDs with LDHs increases the load and speed rate to capture metal cations (Rahmanian et al., 2018b); in a similar way, the experiment in this article aims to improve the load of an anticancer drug. In this paper, we describe the synthesis and characterization of the folate-CDs/DOX/GdDy-LDH nanocomposite as an approach to design a targeted theragnostic agent by combining the magnetic properties of the LDH with the intrinsic fluorescence and targeting properties of folate-CDs, as well as the cytotoxicity of doxorubicin.

The GdDy-LDH was prepared by the conventional precipitation method using the synthesis conditions described by our group (Sánchez Juménez et al., 2020). Briefly, 40 mL of mixed salt solution containing 2.5 mmol of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (Meyer, Mexico), 0.75 mmol of Al(NO3)3·9H2O (Meyer, Mexico), 0.125 mmol of Gd(NO3)3·xH2O (Sigma-Aldrich, USA) and 0.125 mmol of Dy(NO3)3·xH2O (Sigma-Aldrich, USA) was titrated with 6% NH4OH (Meyer, Mexico) under vigorous stirring. The M2+/M3+ molar ratio in this mixture is equal to 2.5:1. The final pH of the solution was adjusted to 8 and the mixture was stirred for 24 h (Fig. 1 ). Finally, the obtained precipitate was washed three times with deionized water and dried at 60 °C.

Folate-CDs were synthesized using 1 mmol of folic acid (Sigma-Aldrich, USA), l -lysine 2 mmol of monohydrochloride (Sigma-Aldrich, USA) and 5 mmol of NaOH (Meyer, Mexico), which were dissolved in 4 mL deionized water. The yellow solution was transferred into a 20 mL Teflon-autoclave at 180 °C for 30 min under vigorous stirring. A dark brown solution was obtained, indicating formation of CDs (Scheme 1b). The solution was centrifuged at 11,000 rpm for 15 min with a HERMLE centrifuge machine (Germany), model Z326, and the supernatant was dried at 80 °C for 1 h.

Folate-CDs/DOX/GdDy-LDH was prepared by the colloidal deposition method. Doxorubicin hydrochloride (0.05 mmol) and folate-CDs (30 mg) were dissolved in 5 mL deionized water, the solution was adjusted to pH 9 with 6% NH4OH and GdDy-LDH (0.1 g) was added (Scheme 1c). The mixture was stirred for 24 h under N2 atmosphere and in the dark. The obtained precipitate was washed several times with deionized water and dried at 60 °C. The supernatant was collected and DOX was quantified by UV–vis spectroscopy at 480 nm (εDOX = 10,400 M−1·cm−1) in a Thermo Scientific Genesys 10S spectrophotometer. The loading of DOX was determined by difference with the supernatant. DOX/GdDy-LDH used as a control in cytotoxicity tests, was also prepared under this methodology, but the addition of Folate-CDs was omitted.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded with a PANalytical Empyrean diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation at 40 kV and 20 mA. Infrared (IR) spectra were collected in a Thermo-Scientific Nicolet iS5 iD5 ATR spectrometer with 32 scans and a resolution of 2 cm−1. The metallic composition was determined with an inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) spectrometer Varian, Vista-MPX CCD simultaneous. The analysis of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were obtained in an SPECS XPS equipment with Al anode and a pass energy of 20 eV. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired with JEOL JEM-1010 with a voltage of 100 kV. Fluorescence spectra were obtained in a F-7000 FL spectrophotometer and the absorbance spectra in a Genesys 10S UV-ViS spectrophotometer. The zeta potential analysis was measured in a Malvern Zetasizer Nano S. The 1H Nuclear Magnetic Resonance spectra was acquired on a Varian Mercury Plus 400 NMR system, using D2O as solvent and tetramethylsilane as internal reference. The SEM images were recorded on a TESCAN MIRA3 LMU scanning electron microscopy. The dispersive spectroscopy detector (EDS) mapping was collected with a detector from Oxford Instruments coupled to a JEOL JSM 5400 LV scanning electron microscope at the Instituto Transdisciplinar de Investigación y Servicios, ITRANS. Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) analyses were performed on the Bruker BioSpec 70/16 scanner with a 7.0 T magnetic field and a pulse sequence RARE-VTR. For T1-relaxivity, measurements were used the following parameters: repetition time (TR) = 40, 100, 200, 500, 1000, 3000, 5000, 7500 ms, echo time (TE) = 11.7 ms, field ofo view (FOV) = 40 × 24 mm2, matrix = 134 × 78 and slice thickness = 2 mm. For T2-relaxivity measurements, the parameters used were TR = 2000 ms, TE = 36 ecos, 40–1440 ms, with a separation between echo times of 40 ms.

The cytotoxicity studies were performed with HeLa cells (ATCC, USA) using the colourimetric WST-1 [2-(4-Iodophenyl)-3-(4-nitrophenyl)-5-(2,4-disulfophenyl)-2H-tetrazolium] cell proliferation assay (Clontech, USA). HeLa cells were seeded in a 96-well plate at a density of 2500 cells/well in DMEM (ATCC) supplemented with 10% FBS (Biowest) and 1% Penicillinn/Streptomycin (Gibco) and cultured at 37 °C, 4% CO2 and 95% RH for 24 h. Afterwards, GdDy-LDH, folate-CDs, DOX and folate-CDs/DOX/GdDy-LDH with different concentrations were added and incubated for 24 h. HeLa cells were washed several times with PBS and 100 μL medium with 2 μL WST-1 was added to each well. After 4 h of incubation, the absorbance was measured at 440 nm (Multiskan, Thermo-Fisher Scientific). Untreated cells were used as a control and the viability was set to 100%. The data obtained were expressed as the mean ± standard error of the mean (n = 3) with a 95% confidence interval.

The XRD pattern of the GdDy-LDH (Fig. 2a) corresponds to the typical crystalline structure of a hydrotalcite-like compound (ICDD card No. 380486). GdDy-LDH have a rhombohedral 3R symmetry, the first two reflections correspond to the (003) and (006) planes. The reflection attributed to (110) plane is not observed around 60° (2theta), indicating the formation of GdDy-LDH structure with low crystallinity. The basal spacing found in this sample (d 003 = 0.89 nm) is characteristic for LDH intercalated with NO3 − anions. The inclusion of lanthanides in this structure is then possible once reflections of secondary phases are not observed.

The FT-IR spectrum shows a broad band at 3400 cm−1 attributed to OH stretching vibration of –OH groups of the sheets and the water molecules. The absorption bands at 1640 corresponds to H-O-H bending vibration of interlayer water (Dinari et al., 2019). In addition, a broad band is observed in the 1340–1390 cm−1 range, the broadening results from the overlapping of two bands: ν(NO3 −) at ~1386 cm-1 and ν(CO32−) at ~1356 cm-1 (Mahjoubi et al., 2015). The band of M-O stretching vibration is observed at around 550 cm−1 (Fig. 2b). The chemical composition of GdDy-LDH was determined by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES) and the results confirm the incorporation of lanthanides into the LDH. The molar fractions in Table 1 indicated that the content of metals is quite close to the nominal amounts used in the synthesis and, in particular, Gd3+and Dy3+ are present in equal amounts.

The trivalent lanthanides provide the paramagnetic properties to the GdDy-LDH, which are required for its use as a contrast agent (Usman et al., 2017; Arratia-Quijada et al., 2016; Zhou and Lu, 2013). The T1-relaxivity plot shows the linear correlation between the longitudinal relaxation rate (1/T1) and the Gd3+ concentration; since the positive contrast improves with the increase of Gd3+ concentration in aqueous suspensions (Fig. 3a). According to r 1 = 3.49 mM−1·s−1 of the GdDy-LDH, this material shows higher contrast effect than the commercial contrast agent Gadovist (r 1 = 2.24 mM−1·s−1), which was used as a control. This could be attributed to the easier access of water molecules to the coordination sphere of the Gd3+ cations, which constitute the sheets of the LDH, thus reducing the longitudinal relaxation time (T1) of the water protons. The influence of dysprosium on the contrast effect of GdDy-LDH was also evaluated by MRI. The Dy3+ has a high magnetic moment, thus decreases the transverse relaxation time (T2) of the protons generating a negative contrast. The T1 effect is therefore negligible (Bottrill et al., 2006). The T2-relaxivity plot shows the proportional increase of transversal relaxation rate (1/T2) with the Dy3+ concentration and a transversal relaxivity of r 2 = 18.17 mM−1·s−1 (Fig. 3b), significantly higher than Sprodiamide, the most-studied Dy3+ complex used as a contrast agent (r 2 = 0.12 mM−1·s−1) (Fowler et al., 2013; Haraldseth et al., 1996). These relaxometries indicate the combination of gadolinium and dysprosium enhances T1- and T2-weighed MRI, which can be useful as a double contrast agent in cardiac imaging (Norek and Peters, 2011).

Folate-CDs were prepared by thermal coupling between folic acid (FA) and l -lysine (Lys) in an alkaline medium. The IR spectrum of the reaction product shows a band at about 1600 cm−1, which is attributed to the stretching vibration of CO amide,(Yang et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2017) and hence confirms the formation of folate-CDs by the condensation between FA and Lys. Moreover, the surface passivation of folate-CDs with amino groups was corroborated by the bands of stretching and bending vibration of NH at 3350 and 1500 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 2b). The XRD pattern displays a broad band centered at 23°(2θ), characteristic for amorphous carbon that constitutes CDs core, and other reflections, which correspond to the NaCl (ICDD card No. 050628) formed by deprotonation of l -lysine monohydrochloride with NaOH during the synthesis. According to transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis, folate-CDs are quasi-spherical nanoparticles, with an average diameter of 8 nm (Fig. 2c).

The surface functionalization of folate-CDs was also analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The spectrum corroborates the presence of C, N and O in folate-CDs, as well as the elements Na and Cl of the NaCl obtained as a secondary product. The C1s band has three bands at 284.9, 286.2 and 288.2 eV, which represent C-C/C=C, C-O/C-N and CO groups, respectively (Fig. 2d) (Yang et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2017). The two bands resolved in N1s spectrum at 399.7 and 401.5 eV correspond to aromatic NC and surface H2N-groups, respectively (Fig. 2e) (Yang et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2017). The presence of folate and Lys on the surface of folate-CDs was also proved by 1H NMR: the spectrum shows the typical signals of hydrogens of the aromatic rings of folate between 8.5 and 6.1 ppm as well as the signals from hydrogens of aliphatic chains of both molecules in the range of 4.4–1.0 ppm (Fig. 1f). Therefore, FTIR, XPS and 1H NMR results confirm passivation of CDs with folate and amino groups from Lys, which impart the hydrophilicity to CDs. Further, the zeta potential of −38.2 mV suggests a high dispersion and stability of the CDs in water. The abundant positive charge on their surface, due to amino groups, generates a strong electrostatic repulsion between the CDs in a suspension (Zhou et al., 2017).

The optical properties of folate-CDs in aqueous solution were explored at room temperature by UV–vis and PL spectroscopy (Fig. 4 ). In the UV–vis spectrum, the absorption band observed at 272 and 350 cm−1 are attributed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic system of the folate-CDs core and of carboxyl-amino groups passivated CDs, respectively (Yang et al., 2017). These functional groups on the surface are electron-donating, thus enhance the conjugation degree of CDs (Li et al., 2014). Photoluminescence spectrum shows a maximum PL emission at 515 nm with an excitation wavelength of 420 nm; this result is desirable to design bioprobes once the irradiation energy is low and the emission in the visible spectra will allow identifying the particles under the optical microscopes. Additionally, the inset in Fig. 4 shows the intense blue emission of the folate-CDs solution when exposed to a UV lamp irradiating light of 365 nm.

Besides the contrast effect in MRI, GdDy-LDH was evaluated as a vehicle for DOX and folate-CDs to explore its possible use for bioimaging and targeted cancer therapy. The folate-CDs/DOX/GdDy-LDH assembly was analyzed by XRD. As shown in Fig. 2a, the reflections of (003) and (006) planes for GdDy-LDH/DOX/folate-CDs are shifted to higher Bragg's angle than those for the pristine GdDy-LDH, indicating the intercalation of carbonate anions during the assembling reaction due to the CO2 contamination and hence the superficial incorporation of DOX and folate-CDs by physisorption. The DOX loading capacity determined by UV–vis was 212.6 μg/g folate-CDs/DOX/GdDy-LDH. The FTIR spectrum also corroborates the functionalization of GdDy-LDH with DOX and folate-CDs. The broad bands at about 3400 and 1650 cm−1 are attributed to OH and NO3 −/CO3 2– stretching vibrations,(Mahjoubi et al., 2015; Kloprogge et al., 2002) respectively, which is typical of pristine LDH. The bands in the range of 1600–1500 cm−1 were assigned to the NH and CO stretching vibrations (Fig. 2b), which are functional groups of DOX and folate-CDs (Kong et al., 2018) suggesting its physisorption on the GdDy-LDH. According to Rahmanian et al.,(Rahmanian et al., 2018b) a shift of the CO band can suggest a strong electrostatic interaction between CDs with negative surface charge and LDH layers with positive surface charge, or the formation of a C-O-M species. However, a shift of the CO stretching mode is not observed in the folate-CDs/DOX/GdDy-LDH spectrum due to the overlap of the characteristic CO bands of DOX and the folate-CDs. Likewise, Hakeem et al (Hakeem et al., 2018). reported that a hydrophobic region of DOX is formed in the layers, favoring the drug incorporation by hydrophobic interactions. EDS mapping analysis for folate-CDs/DOX/GdDy-LDH shows that Zn, Al, Gd, Dy, C, O and N are homogeneously distributed along the particles (Fig. 5a-h ). Furthermore, the DOX and folate-CDs assembly on the LDH reduces its zeta potential from 51.9 to 39.7 mV, due to the drug hydrophobicity. Nonetheless, the folate-CDs/DOX/GdDy-LDH has good water dispersibility and a particle size of about 137 nm (Fig. 5i), which are desirable to design biomedical materials.

Cytotoxic activity of folate-CDs/DOX/GdDy-LDH was studied by WST-1 assay using HeLa cell line, which overexpresses the folate receptor. Cell viability was maintained above 80% after 24 h of incubation with GdDy-LDH and folate-CDs at concentrations as high as 500 μg/mL (Fig. 6 ), demonstrating the good biocompatibility of the carrier and target agent. Subsequently, HeLa cells were treated with DOX, DOX/GdDy-LDH and folate-CDs/DOX/GdDy-LDH with equivalent drug concentrations from 0.04 to 1.25 μg/mL for 24 h (Fig. 6b). The viability of the cells decreases with increasing DOX concentrations for all three cases. However, the viability of DOX/DyGd-LDH is lower than DOX, indicating that the vehicle promotes the efficiency of the treatment. The folate-CDs/DOX/GdDy-LDH exhibit a half-maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) of 0.16 μg/mL, which is lower than of DOX itself (1.16 μg/mL) or DOX/GdDy-LDH (0.9 μg/mL). Therefore, cytotoxic assays evidence the effective release of DOX in conditions that approximate a tumor microenvironment,(Wang et al., 2013; Choi and Choy, 2011b) such as the presence of other ions, biomolecules and cellular metabolism products, which may influence the release of DOX. Moreover, this result indicates that the cytotoxic efficacy of folate-CDs/DOX/GdDy-LDH is enhanced due to functionalization with folate-CDs, suggesting a more efficient internalization promoted by the electrostatic interaction between the positive charge of the nanocomposite and endocytosis mediated by the folate receptor (Yang et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2017).

The folate-CDs/DOX/GdDy-LDH nanocomposite was prepared by colloidal deposition of folate-CDs and DOX onto GdDy-LDH. The incorporation of gadolinium and dysprosium by partial isomorphic substitution of Al3+ sites produces an LDH structure with an MRI contrast effect. GdDy-LDH promotes better T1 and T2-weighted MR imaging than Gadovist, a commercial MRI contrast agent. In addition, GdDy-LDH has low cytotoxicity, good water dispersibility and high loading capacity. Folate-CD/DOX/GdDy-LDH nanocomposite exhibits enhanced cytotoxicity in HeLa cells, 7.2 and 5.6-fold higher than free DOX and DOX-LDH, respectively. The superficial incorporation of folate-CDs enables an efficient DOX delivery to folate receptor-positive cancer cells. The amino groups found in the lysine moiety improve an unspecific adhesion to cell membranes due to electrostatic attraction, while the folate groups promote the folate-receptor mediated endocytosis. Therefore, we demonstrated the promising features of the CDs/GdDy-LDH assembly to design theragnostic systems for bimodal imaging and targeted therapy.

K.N.A. thanks CONACYT for the scholarship. This work was supported by the Consejo Nacional de Ciencia y Tecnología (CONACyT) project 256690 (Fondo Sectorial de Investigación para la Educación). The authors thank the access to the Imaging facilities at the National Laboratory for Magnetic Resonance in Medicine (LANIREM, CONACyT, UNAM, UAQ, CIMAT), and the technical assistance of Juan Ortiz and Luis Concha. Elena Smolentseva is acknowledged for the ICP-OES analyses at the Centro de Nanociencias y Nanotecnología (CNYN, UNAM) and Prakhar Sengar to acquire the PL spectra. Also, the authors thanks to Armando Rentería Ruiz for the SEM images.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
